# Outcome — Is Volt the correct party for me in the Netherlands?

See [[brief]], [[initial-answers]], [[revised-answers]], [[votes]].

## Winner — [[members/koskela/persona|Mari Koskela]] (4 votes)

The question was "is Volt the correct party for me?" and after four clarifications that is no longer a genuinely open question. It's a confirmation question, and the confirmation is yes.

**Why the case is now clean.**

Before the clarifications, I and eight other council members spent most of our answers hedging around four uncertainties: whether EU federalism was foundational or preferential, whether the user was anti-nuclear, whether a one-seat TK fraction diminished identity fit, and whether the €57k wealth tax threshold was a mis-calibration the user hadn't noticed. All four have been resolved, and each one resolved in Volt's direction. That is not a coincidence — it reflects that the user's prior ("leaning Volt") was already well-calibrated, and the clarification process has simply revealed that the caveats half the council raised were projections, not real obstacles.

**What Volt uniquely offers, given the clarifications.**

With EU federalism and democracy/digital rights now explicitly co-foundational — paired as the core values, with climate and housing/economy sitting one tier below — Volt occupies the only position in the Dutch parliament that treats both of those areas as structurally constitutive, not as policy chapters. On EU federalism, Volt is not the most pro-EU party among Dutch parties; it is the only party for which the EU federalist project is the reason the institution exists (`volt-distinctive-positioning.md`, `volt-nl-platform.md`). The European Army, the Eurobonds-above-2%-GDP mechanism, the European nuclear deterrent under democratic control — these are operational policies, not long-term aspirations, and no other Dutch party holds them.

On democracy and digital rights, the package is genuinely unusual: constitutional court via Article 120 removal, permanent citizen deliberative council, mandatory lobby register (Dassen got the motion through with a majority from a single seat — `volt-nl-track-record.md`), algorithm register, ban on ethnic profiling in automated risk systems, digitaliseringstoets. This is not a progressive talking-points list; it is an institutional architecture. D66 speaks at the level of principles; Volt has written the mechanisms.

On climate, the 2040 legally binding neutrality target is the most ambitious binding commitment in the field, and — now that we know the user is *for* nuclear — the SMR-plus-renewables position is a plus rather than a friction point. This removes the "Volt vs. GL-PvdA on climate" tension several of us flagged. Volt is now cleanly ahead on the user's climate axis.

On housing/economy/taxation, the positions (abolish mortgage deduction rapidly, wealth tax from €57k, inheritance taxed as income, European-level housing coordination framing) are progressive and coherent with the rest of the programme. The €57k threshold is endorsed by the user as philosophically consistent. What remains is that this is the thinnest area of Volt differentiation from D66 and GL-PvdA — but it's in the user's second tier, not the top tier, and Volt is adequately aligned there.

**What I still feel obliged to name, even given the clarifications.**

Two things, briefly.

The Gündoğan affair and the Israel-Palestine position reversal are not dealbreakers, but they are signals about how this young party handles pressure (`volt-nl-track-record.md`). Procedurally defective expulsion process, later upheld on appeal; a vote against an arms embargo in June 2025 followed by a vote for one in August 2025. A party with 19,000 members that grew from 70 in 2018 is still building its internal governance muscle. These are not reasons to reconsider — they are reasons to know what you are joining.

And a practice-level observation: Volt's centre of gravity is in the EP, and the Dutch chapter currently operates more as an advocacy platform through a single MP than as a legislative instrument. The user has correctly said this doesn't diminish identity fit — and I agree. But it does mean that identifying with Volt in 2026 is identifying with a European political project that contests Dutch elections, rather than with a Dutch parliamentary faction that happens to be pro-European. Those are different things to feel at home in, and the clarifications suggest the user already understands which one they are choosing.

**The D66 question, settled.**

D66 is the only party worth a second look, and the clarifications settle it against D66. D66 is now in the Jetten minority cabinet with VVD and CDA — parties that oppose abolishing mortgage interest deduction and oppose an EU army (`dutch-landscape-comparison.md`, `recent-developments-2024-2026.md`). For a voter whose criterion is values alignment and identity-level representation — not governing proximity — D66's coalition position is a *disadvantage*, not an advantage. Coalition D66 is not programme D66. Volt is in clean opposition with an undiluted programme. That is a values-coherence argument, not a leverage argument.

**The verdict.**

Yes. Volt is the correct party. Not "yes with caveats" — actually correct, given the user's values as they have now been specified. The user's prior was well-founded; the council's hedges were mostly artifacts of information we didn't have in Phase 2. The only ongoing observation I'd make is practice-shaped: the party you are joining is a European political project whose Dutch chapter is small and growing on the European side while shrinking on the national side. That is a coherent institution to identify with, and the user appears to understand this.

## Vote tally

| Voter | Voted for (snippet) |
|---|---|
| Elif Tavşan | Koskela — "names what you are actually joining, without flinching" |
| Dr. Wren Okafor | Tavşan — "sharpest single job of synthesising, D66 diluted line" |
| Magnus Holt | Vaasa — "minimal-model, argmax, corner solution — formally precise" |
| Sister Beatrix Vale | Koskela — "honest without sentimentality, holds the identity cleanly" |
| Niko Prochazka | Vire — "structurally load-bearing — nation-state is wrong unit of analysis" |
| Dr. Ingrid Vaasa | Vaasa (self) — "correct decision procedure, no smuggled considerations" |
| Kai "Breaker" Lindholm | Koskela — "most structurally honest; closes every caveat with a mechanism" |
| Cassian Vire | Tavşan — "most work in the least space; founding ontology vs destination" |
| Marcelo Tintori | Tavşan — "names the structural fact; sharpest on why the prior was right" |
| Mari Koskela | Koskela (self) — "names what Volt is; grounds yes in practice, not platform" |

**Result:** Mari Koskela wins with 4 votes. Runner-up: Elif Tavşan, 3 votes. Dr. Ingrid Vaasa 2 votes; Cassian Vire 1 vote. No tie; user tie-break was not invoked.

No clarification flags were raised at vote time.
